issue_title: 新版企业微信获取ACCESSTOKEN时功能不足
issue_body:
微信现在正将企业号全面升级为企业微信，新申请的企业号都会作为企业微信存在，但接口上作了小的调整，其中最重要的是调整了access_token的获取方式。
新的access_token在不同的应用中都不相同，而不是像之前只有一个，现在是根据：

- corpid
- secret 指应用的secret

那在缓存的时候也需要针对不同的应用进行缓存，目前的access_token_key是这样的，会导致不同的应用仍然获取了同一个值，从而导致失败：
`
    @property
    def access_token_key(self):
        return '{0}_access_token'.format(self.appid)
`
能否针对企业微信做些变更，可以把key值变为self.appid + appsecret[10:]这样的方式

Am I understanding this library correctly?

issue_title: Making a deep copy of a backend resets the `id`
issue_body:
On `develop`:
```python
import time
j = Job()
j.submit()
time.sleep(2) #Wait for job to start
print j.backend.id
f = j.copy()
print j.backend.id
```
will print
```
84286
-1
```
and so for some reason, copying the job has reset the `id` of the job's backend to the default value. I found this behaviour since it happens during a small step of the copy process, when the backend is deep-copied:
```python
import time, copy
j = Job()
j.submit()
time.sleep(2) #Wait for job to start
print j.backend.id
b = copy.deepcopy(j.backend)
print j.backend.id
```
It should be noted that `b.id` and `f.backend.id` are all set to `-1` as well so the information just seems to be being lost entirely.

Looking at the tests now, it seems it's failing on [Savannah8009](http://ganga.web.cern.ch/ganga/release/6.1.14-pre/reports/latest/output/Ganga.test.Bugs.Savannah8009__localxml.out) which was the test I was converting when I came across this.

issue_title: Subjob marked as dirty makes all subjobs get flushed
issue_body:
At present, according to `flush` in SubJobXMLList.py, if the parent job is marked as dirty (which it will be if any subjobs change) then ALL the subjobs get flushed. This can be an expensive operation if there are a lot of subjobs which could make the monitoring go very slowly.

Ideally, it should only flush dirty subjobs, not all of them.

issue_title: SSDP suffers from multibyte string decode problem
issue_body:
It seems there are several other problems with multibyte string decoding and encoding in this package. Let me add my $0.0000000000002 :smiley:.

Some device in my network has an SSDP response that blows up `ssdp.py` discovery `sock.recv().decode("utf-8")` with `UnicodeDecodeError: 'utf-8' codec can't decode byte 0x90 in position 5: invalid start byte`. Since I cannot control all devices and since this is really a robustness issue, I tried replacing the bad UTF-8 with the default replacement by changing the code to
```
                    response = sock.recv(1024).decode("utf-8", "replace")
```
This solves my problem by allowing the discovery to continue even after a response from such an evil device is found. It seems harmless to do this.

issue_title: R200 1080p synchronization issue
issue_body:
| Required Info  |    |
|---|---|
| Camera Model | R200 | 
| Firmware Version |  1.0.72.06   | 
| Operating System & Version |  Ubuntu  16.04 LTS |
| Kernel Version (Linux Only)  |  4.4.0-72 |
| Build System | Makefile |

I have been analysing the delay between frames of my R200 and I have found that the signal has a considerable amount of jitter. If the colour stream is set at 480p, this effect is delimited in an interval of (-1,1) ms but, with the 1080p colour configuration, the frame delay varies along a (-3,3) ms.

As this R200 device is being used along with other kind of depth sensors, in order to get a generic timestamp reference, I am using std::chrono::high_resolution_clock timestamps just after getting a new frame with wait_for_frames() method.

You can found one example of these results below.

1. When the camera is configured at:

- Color: 640x480@30fps bgr8 format

- Depth: 640x480@30fps z16 format

the difference between frame timestamps plot looks like this:
![selfdelayr200-480](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/28562935/25856397/270b9c2e-34d6-11e7-9c6c-4c527cf04321.png)


2. If the camera configuration is changed to: 

- Color: 1920x1080@30fps bgr8 format

- Depth: 640x480@30fps z16 format

the plot looks like that:
![selfdelayr200-1080](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/28562935/25856410/2f6bf076-34d6-11e7-9abc-2dd4e471d374.png)

As it can be seen, in the 480p configuration the timestamp values are much more synchronized than in the 1080p case. Could it be happening because of an excessive computational burden in the device?

My current code is the following:

`pipeline = rs.pipeline()` 
`config = rs.config()`

`config.enable_record_to_file('test.bag')`
`pipeline.start(config)`  
`try:`  
    `start = time.time()  `
    `while time.time()-start < 20:`  
         `pipeline.wait_for_frames().keep()`  
`finally:  `
    `pipeline.stop()  `

Is there a way to avoid this? Or is there a better way to write to a file in general?

issue_title: Feature: Java Bindings
issue_body:
Hello RealSense enthousiats, 

I developped a first Java binding for libRealsense: 

- The bindings are generated by [JavaCPP](https://github.com/bytedeco/javacpp). 
- They will be part of [JavaCPP-Presets](https://github.com/bytedeco/javacpp-presets), for now they are waiting in a pull request from my [fork](https://github.com/poqudrof/javacpp-presets/tree/realsense-pull). 
- They work with OpenCV in Java through JavaCV. 
- They will work with [Processing](https://processing.org/) and be part of the [PapARt library](https://project.inria.fr/papart/) . 

It is a first version, not a full feature yet. I can get color and depth, IR is also theoritically available.

If anyone want to contribute you are welcome to do so, here is what is missing: 
- Linux only.

issue_title: Discussion: default n_jobs for Featurizers
issue_body:
Currently the default n_jobs for featurizers is 1, i.e., serial jobs.:

matminer/featurizers/base.py:105

This choice is in-line with what sklearn featurizers do, e.g.:

http://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.ensemble.RandomForestClassifier.html

I'd suggest being more aggressive and setting this equal to cpu_count() - i.e., to use full parallelization by default. For example, the multiprocessing module uses cpu_count when the number of processes is set as ``None`` (default is full parallelization).

Regardless of the default, I can see use cases for both and it would be nice to have a "set-it-once-and-forget-it" way to configure n_jobs. e.g., a config variable one can set at the beginning of a Jupyter notebook.

issue_title: Provide wheel distribution on PyPI
issue_body:
Hi!

Please could this package also by made available as a wheel on PyPI?
https://pypi.python.org/pypi/graphql-core/1.1

Wheels have the following advantages:
* Faster installation time
* Allows the wheel cache to work even when using the new pip hash-checking mode
* Allows tools to statically inspect the package metadata without having to execute untrusted code.
* ...and more - see: http://pythonwheels.com

This package is pure Python and supports both Python 2 and 3, so in theory could be distributed as a "universal wheel":
https://packaging.python.org/tutorials/distributing-packages/#wheels

...however I notice that there's some `__builtin__` vs `builtins` workaround going on here:
https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-core/blob/f983e19cc8cd818275621e2909fb485cf227fe0c/setup.py#L5-L17

...which I believe means separate wheels have to be generated for each of Python 2 and 3. 

The Python packaging guide recommends using [Twine](https://github.com/pypa/twine) to upload packages:
https://packaging.python.org/tutorials/distributing-packages/#uploading-your-project-to-pypi

As such, the steps for building/uploading a new release are then:

```bash
$ pip2 install -U twine setuptools wheel
$ pip3 install -U setuptools wheel
$ rm -rf dist/*
$ python2 setup.py sdist bdist_wheel
$ python3 setup.py bdist_wheel
$ twine upload dist/*
```

The PyPI package listing will then display three entries for the new release, one with type "Source" and two of type "Python Wheel" (one for each of Python 2 and 3).

Many thanks :-)

issue_title: Trusted users concept does not work with certain settings.
issue_body:
As the title says, trusted users do not work anymore after 2.2.1 to 2.2.3 upgrade. This is a result of the caching system. 

Assuming AXES_LOCK_OUT_BY_COMBINATION_USER_AND_IP is False, the function `get_cache_key` does not look at username anymore. This breaks the whole concept of trusted users. As the cache keys are based on ip's only. I'm not sure if this was intended or a bug but it sure is not backwards compatible. 

is_already_locked should not look in the cache but just get user attempts instead.

issue_title: Documentation on Collector behaviour needs clarification
issue_body:
After grappling with some speed issues in develop, I realised that Collector.collect() was being run for every pipeline tag in my project. Eventually I narrowed my issue down to this:

https://github.com/jazzband/django-pipeline/blob/master/pipeline/collector.py#L31-L32

`request` was always `None` for me, and so the solution was to add `'django.core.context_processors.request'` to my template's `context_processors` option which gave me a roughly 6x speedup.

I imagine it's probably enough to just add something to the documentation which instructs users to add the context processor if they intend to use multiple tags, although I'm not sure where. Installation->Recommendations could work, or maybe near the top of the Usage or Configuration sections.

issue_title: sys.prefix vs. other WD options for tests
issue_body:
Some tests were set up to use sys.prefix as the working directory because that is where the pip installation puts all the data files.  That was so that someone with the pip installation who has not downloaded from Github could run tests and make sure everything was working correctly on their machine, and also so that I could test the pip installation directly.  Using cwd + 'data_files' is going to break that functionality.  Here's an explanation about how that works with pip: [stack overflow](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/7934162/making-a-python-package-for-pip-with-some-data-files) 

However, using sys.prefix doesn't work correctly if you haven't installed using pip (or copied the data_files folder to sys.prefix + pmagpy_data_files).  Perhaps we can discuss how and where we want the tests to be able to run?  I like having the ability to directly test the pip installation, so maybe we could figure out how to get it to work both ways?

Also, quite a few tests are now failing for me.  'pmagpy_data_files' was not changed to 'data_files' everywhere, so a bunch of tests can't find test data.  Let's decide how we want to do things and get this fixed.    

@Caoimhinmg, @Swanson-Hysell ?

so we were not checking this on Dec 12, 2016

In this paper: https://arxiv.org/pdf/1707.03905.pdf , the authors talk about selecting multiplier for minority classes, which I read as instead of say oversampling to match the number of instances of the majority class, just oversample to some fraction of the number of instances of the majority class. Is there an easy way to do this with the random oversampler?

Hi,
I tried to upload binary data to Report Portal but faced with issues that python client does not provide method to perform this.
Following class implemented but not exported:
```
class File(RQ):
    def __init__(self, name, content):
        super(File, self).__init__()
        self.name = name
        self.content = content
```
As a result, service module does not implement methods for uploading binary data. It uses only following REST API endpoints:
 - launch
 - item

Have to mention here, I did not found REST endpoint for posting binary data using the latest version from docker hub.

issue_title: talisker > 0.6.7 does not work with celery 3.1.23
issue_body:
We had deployed delta-service with talisker 0.6.7 and celery 3.1.23. This combination was working fine. Upon upgrading to talisker 0.9.0, we can now no longer queue tasks:

https://sentry.ols.staging.canonical.com/canonical/delta-service/issues/43/

The version of celery has not changed. Downgrading talisker to 0.6.7 fixes the problem.

celery 4.0 is out now, but the talisker docs do not mention whether 4.x is supported.

issue_title: Not finding vhosts on A{mazon,rch}Linux
issue_body:
## My operating system is (include version):

Amazon Linux 2017.03

## I installed Certbot with (certbot-auto, OS package manager, pip, etc):

certbot-auto

## I ran this command and it produced this output:

```
sudo env PATH=$PATH ./certbot-auto --apache --debug -v -d example.org
...
Could not reverse map the HTTPS VirtualHost to the original
...
```

## Certbot's behavior differed from what I expected because:

Certbot should be able to find the vhost.

This issue was created in response to https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/rate-limited-after-trying-multiple-solutions-to-could-not-reverse-map-the-https-virtualhost-to-the-original-issue/37252. Adding `--apache-vhost-root` causes the "Could not reverse map..." error message to go away and be replaced by:

> No vhost exists with servername or alias of: example.org (or it's in a file with multiple vhosts, which Certbot can't parse yet). No vhost was selected. Please specify ServerName or ServerAlias in the Apache config, or split vhosts into separate files.

I played with this myself for a bit in Docker using http24 installed in the default paths and even with `--apache-vhost-root`, we're not finding the vhost. The return value from `certbot_apache.configurator.get_virtual_hosts()` is an empty list.

@joohoi, since Amazon Linux is Red Hat based, this might be a good test to see how better support for non-Debian systems is coming in Apache.

issue_title: Certbot-auto not generating certificates for nginx.
route53: Bring back parallel waiting for propagation
issue_body: The route53 plugin used to be able to send several update requests, and wait on them all in parallel. It looks like we lost that ability in https://github.com/certbot/certbot/pull/4771, possibly due to a bad rebase. We should bring back that capability. Waiting for the server to declare that it's finished propagating is very slow, and so parallelizing that waiting speeds things up a *lot*.

issue_title: Implement a Lexicon-based DNS authenticator for NameSilo
issue_body: Implement an Authenticator plugin which can fulfill a dns-01 challenge using Lexicon.

issue_title: "DNS problem: networking error looking up A" for CNAME?
issue_body:
I have two hostnames with very similar configurations. Both are hosted on the same nameservers, both have a CNAME to a Heroku hostname. Which then, itself, resolves to a second CNAME before finally an A is found.

The first, `www.toicon.com` worked as expected.

The second, `inside.theartificial.nl` complains:

> DNS problem: networking error looking up A 

Is there something magic about `www` hostnames that make CNAME acceptable, or is my simple domain somehow misconfigured?

issue_title: Did the sf.limits() method get removed?
issue_body:
There used to be a method called `limits` to fetch Salesforce limits. This one.

```
def limits(self):
        """Returns API limit object
        """
        url = self.base_url + "limits"
        result = self._call_salesforce('GET', url)
        if result.status_code != 200:
            raise SalesforceGeneralError(url,
                                         'limits',
                                         result.status_code,
                                         result.content)
        json_result = result.json(object_pairs_hook=OrderedDict)
        if len(json_result) == 0:
            return None
        else:
            return json_result
```

I just upgraded to the new version, and it no longer exists. I might have been using an old version(since we use it on Appengine, we store the module directly in lib/ and i don't remember what version we installed, and it's not mentioned in `__init__.py` and we didn't store setup.py in lib/)

We were using that API to fetch remaining api calls while syncing tasks, events. Is there a particular commit i can look at to see why it was removed or refactored?

Also the limit() on the older version still works for us.

issue_title: Differentiate "address in use" between API and Raiden service port
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

As reported in #1032 the CLI currently doesn't differentiate between the API and Raiden service when reporting an `Address already in use` error. [The error handler](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/03a05586f039e544d2d8ceb42307fe02e29aad15/raiden/ui/cli.py#L522) blindly assumes that the conflict was caused by the Raiden service.

## Solution

Catch the socket error at a lower level and convert it to a specific one that allows the CLI level to differentiate between API and Raiden service.

## Tasklist

- [ ] Catch `socket.error` `EADDRINUSE` nearer to opening the ports and reraise as app specific exceptions
- [ ] Provide an appropriate error message for each case
- [ ] Add a changelog entry.

issue_title: User policies
issue_body:
Implement a policy class that controls some of the node's behavior:

- Decide what to do once a new channel is openned
  - Ignore a channel from a unknown peer?
  - Automatically add to the available graph and use it?
- Decide how to deposit asset into a channel
  - When a channel is opened, should a deposit be made?
     - A minimum deposit
     - A matching deposit
- If there are no available channels to do a mediated transfer, should a new deposit be made?
  - New channel, to whom?
  - Existing channel, which?
- Control the transfer finalization
  - Should a Secret revealed right away?
  - Should a RequestSecret be sent right away?

An alternative implementation should expose callbacks for these decisions, this can be used to trigger UI changes and request the user's input.

issue_title: Task status ambiguities
issue_body:
With the new `new` status, we now have

* `new`: new task, not submitted, marked by the absense of runtime information
* `pending`: touched by task engine and ready to be submitted, marked by extra runtime information
* `submitted`: submitted to PBS, marked by existence of `.sh` and `.job_id` files
* `running`: task running, marked by existence of `.pulse` files
* `failed`:  task failed, marked by `ret_code` in result
* `canceled`: task cancelled from outside, marked by readonly `.pulse` file
* `signature-mismatch`: task completed but signature does not match
* `completed`: task completed

Whereas most statuses are essential, I am wondering if we should
* merge `pending` and `submitted` because they are both `pending`: The difference is that `pending` is sos pending and `submitted` is PBS pending.
* merge `signature-mismatch` to `failed`: The difference here is that a task might be `completed` and then becomes `signature-mismatch` when the output files have been changed externally. This can be confusing because the "result" is meant to be moved around and archived, sometimes without original output files. It make more sense to stop checking signatures after the task has been completed. Note that the new cache mechanism does not check signature so the old behavior is not working correctly anyway.


issue_title: Use obj_or_id more widely to make APIs more flexible
issue_body:
`canvasapi.util.obj_or_id` makes it easier to implement flexible methods that accept either an `int` ID or an object that has an `id` attribute. This could be used more widely than it is right now. Just to give a few examples:

- `Canvas.get_course_nickname(self, course_id)` could accept a `Course` object for `course_id`.

- `Section.get_submission(self, assignment_id, user_id, **kwargs)` could accept an `Assignment` object for `assignment_id`, and could accept a `User` object for `user_id`.

- `Course.conversations_batch_update(self, conversation_ids, event)` could accept a list of `Conversation` objects for `conversation_ids`. Note that allowing this would require applying `obj_or_id` to all values in a list rather than merely a single value.

issue_title: Resolving inconsistencies in the API.
issue_body:
Right now, there are several inconsistencies in the API. Some of the following may be fine.

1. We change the way that we call remote functions (versus regular functions), that is `f.remote()` versus `f()`. But we do not change the way we instantiate actors (versus regular classes), both are just `Class()`.

2. We change the way we call remote functions (versus regular functions), that is `f.remote()` versus `f()`, but we do not change the way we call actor methods (versus regular class methods). Both are `c.method()`.

3. The word `remote` is an adjective, but `actor` is a noun.

There are a handful of options.

A. (addresses 1 and 3) Replace `@ray.remote` and `@ray.actor` with `@ray.task` and `@ray.actor` respectively. Call remote functions with `f.remote()`. Create actors with `Class.remote()`.

B. (addresses 1 and 3) Replace both `@ray.remote` and `@ray.actor` with `@ray.remote`. Call remote functions with `f.remote()`. Create actors with `Class.remote()`.

C. (addresses 1 and 3) Replace both `@ray.remote` and `@ray.actor` with `@ray.remote`. Call remote functions with `f.task()`. Create actors with `Class.actor()`. 

Note that none of these address problem (2). The issue with (2) is that calling `c.method.remote()` or something like that is kind of verbose. There are probably good options that I'm not thinking of right now.

cc @jacobandreas @pcmoritz @istoica

issue_title: Handle recursive objects or throw an exception.
issue_body:
The following leads to an infinite loop (and hence a segfault).

```python
import ray
ray.init(start_ray_local=True, num_workers=0)

l = []
l.append(l)
ray.put(l)  # This causes a segmentation fault.
```

The way we serialize objects currently does not allow for recursive objects.

issue_title: Some sketch of how to use settings to provide an API object to the handlers
issue_body:
Let's say you have a Python `Git` class that has methods such as `status`, `add`, etc. that talk to git using the command line. You want to use that class in all your handlers.

In your server extension code you will instantiate one of these `Git` objects:

```python
def load_jupyter_server_extension(nbapp):
    git = Git() # The Python class all of your handlers will need/use
    nbapp.web_app.settings['git'] = git
    setup_handlers(nbapp.web_app)
```

Then to access that from your handlers, you will want a property on all your handlers (in a common base class:

```python
@property
def git(self):
    return self.settings['git']
```

Then in your actual handler methods you can use this instance, like this:

```python

class StatusHandler(BaseHandler):

    def get(self):
        result = self.git.status(..)

```

issue_title: Setting the layout overflow alone does not work
issue_body: ```python from ipywidgets import * VBox([IntSlider(), IntSlider()], layout={'overflow': 'hidden', 'width':'200px', 'height':'50px'}) ``` gives (notice the overflow is auto, the default, not 'hidden' like we wanted) The code at https://github.com/jupyter-widgets/ipywidgets/blob/34091c58f5f21a947503031d48f623a23fa58b86/packages/base/src/widget_layout.ts#L100 means that having both `overflow`, `overflow_x`, and `overflow_y` traits means they stomp on each other, i.e., overflow gets correctly set to 'auto', but then `overflow_x` and `overflow_y` being nothing erases the overflow value. CC @SylvainCorlay. I think we should remove the `overflow` as a synced attribute, but for backwards compatibility we can make it a non-synced property that automatically sets `overflow_x` and `overflow_y`. Thoughts?

issue_title: Allow removing None field as optinal

issue_title: Validity of the sampling method on files with large numbers of entities ?
issue_body:
From my understanding, to provide pairs to label, dedupe choses among a subset of 5000 pairs which were generated so as to uniformly cover all simple (one field) predicates. If working on a large file with a large number of distinct entities, I would suspect that choosing these pairs simply by randomly picking records with a simple predicate in common might yield a very small number of actual matches (if any) in the sample to label, since the probability of having two distinct entities with a predicate in common becomes larger...

I am missing something? I this issue already dealt with somehow in the code? If not, do you have an intuition on how to fix this (I would suspect increasing sample size or blocking on multiple fields could become necessary as the number of distinct entities grows...) ?

issue_title: Updates not recognized in 1.0.0
issue_body:
Before 1.0.0 I was updating attributes with the v4 API on both merge requests and hooks and able to save them correctly as the manager recognized I had modified them and was sending the updates to the API. But after 1.0.0 it appears the UpdateMixin (I assume, not familiar with this code) doesn't recognize changes any longer, which will cause the `save()` calls to fail when Gitlab returns a 400 because it wasn't actually asked to change anything.

Here's what my code looked like before
```py
if not has_valid_changelog:
    self.log.info(f"Adding CHANGELOG label to {merge_req.web_url}")

    if 'CHANGELOG' in merge_req.labels:
        self.log.info(f"MR {merge_req.web_url} is already tagged with CHANGELOG label")
        return

    merge_req.labels.append('CHANGELOG')
    merge_req.save()
```
And after
```py
if not has_valid_changelog:
    self.log.info(f"Adding CHANGELOG label to {merge_req.web_url}")

    if 'CHANGELOG' in merge_req.labels:
        self.log.info(f"MR {merge_req.web_url} is already tagged with CHANGELOG label")
        return

    merge_req.labels.append('CHANGELOG')
    # Need to stringize, since this doesn't go through the usual route
    setattr(merge_req, 'labels', ','.join(merge_req.labels))
    merge_req.save()
```

issue_title: Identity for features generated in BlockAggregate?
issue_body:
**I certify I'm including:**

- [x] A descriptive title
- [x] A description of the problem I'm trying to solve, including *why* I think this is a problem
- [ ] An overview of the suggested solution
- [ ] If the feature changes current behaviour, reasons why my solution is better

### Scenario

We serve different difficulty modes for a game. A user always plays in the same difficulty model.

We we want to predict how many purchases the user will make.

### Events

Every time the user plays a round, we receive an event of this shape:

| user_id | country | purchase | difficulty_mode | 
|--------|---------|-------------------|-----------------|
|ht83nx|US|true|3|

### Transformation

| user_id | country | total_purchases | difficulty_mode | total_rounds_played |
|--------|---------|-----------------|-----------------|---------------------|
|ht83nx|US|12|3|410|

### DTC?

This is a very naive, but potential scenario. In order to execute this transformation theoretically  the operations supported by a `BlockAggregate` in a Streaming DTC should suffice.

But we also need to indicate that `user_id` is the Identity for such features. So far, we set the Identity in a Streaming DTC so it's used subsequently in a Window DTC. The output of a BlockAggregate won't have unique entries by Identity. 

But this is a different case. We require the Identity to be unique, so we can use the result for prediction.

How could we approach this problem? is there anything missing?

issue_title: Output shape discrepancy between keras.applications.ResNet50 and original paper
issue_body:
In the tables of the original paper (and in other implementations [official tensorflow](https://github.com/tensorflow/models/blob/master/official/resnet/resnet_model.py#L518), [torchvision](https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/master/torchvision/models/resnet.py#L105), [keras-resnet](https://github.com/raghakot/keras-resnet/blob/master/resnet.py#L212), [tensorpack](https://github.com/tensorpack/tensorpack/blob/master/examples/ResNet/resnet_model.py#L120)) the output shape after the initial 3x3 max pooling is `56x56` but in this ResNet50 implementation the output shape is `55x55`.

I tried to add `padding=same` to [this line](https://github.com/keras-team/keras-applications/blob/master/keras_applications/resnet50.py#L233) but it looks like the provided imagenet weights were trained for the `55x55` output shape.

issue_title: Replacing `if variable not in dict` construction
issue_body:
Hello, firstly I would like to thank your for this new library which removes the burden from writing some repetitive code, specially regarding text, and let us focus on solving problems instead.

While reading `text.py`, I spotted the following construction in two different places:
```python
if variable not in some_dict:
    some_dict[variable] = 1
else:
    some_dict[variable] += 1
```

If `some_dict = defaultdict(int)`, then this code could be replaced by the one-liner `some_dict[variable] += 1`. Why not use it? According to the tests below it is even faster:

```python
In [1]: from collections import defaultdict;
In [2]: simple_dict = dict()
In [3]: def fun(): z = defaultdict(int); z['shoe'] += 1;

# >> Inserting a new element
In [4]: %timeit if 'shoe' not in simple_dict: simple_dict['shoe'] = 1
# 56.6 ns ± 0.0336 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10000000 loops each)

In [5]: %timeit fun
# 36.1 ns ± 0.00178 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10000000 loops each)

# >> Fetching existing element
In [6]: %timeit simple_dict['shoe']
# 54.3 ns ± 0.012 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10000000 loops each)

In [7] ddict = defaultdict(int); ddict['shoe'] += 1
In [8]: %timeit ddict['shoe']
# 58.3 ns ± 0.0122 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10000000 loops each)

# >> Updating existing element:
In [9]: def fun_2():
     ...:     if 'shoe' not in simple_dict: simple_dict['shoe'] = 1
     ...:     else: simple_dict['shoe'] += 1
     ...:   
In [10]: %timeit fun_2()
# 290 ns ± 0.171 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1000000 loops each)

In [11]: z = defaultdict(int)
In [12]: %timeit z['shoe'] += 1
# 122 ns ± 0.0166 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10000000 loops each)
```

issue_title: Parse time increases significantly for higher fixed repeat values
issue_body:
Hi,

I'm noticing an issue regarding parse time for higher fixed repeat values. The grammar I'm using is dynamically generated and used for matching spoken commands. One example:

rule_code: w3
rule_media: w6
rule_bash: w9
rule_terminal: w12
rule_optional_number: g15
rule_cmdprompt: w21
rule_windows: g24
r28: (("start" "excel"))
r32: (("start" "terminal"))
w3: "code"
w6: "media"
w9: "bash"
w12: "terminal"
g15: ("0" | "1" | "2")~0..1
w21: "command" "prompt"
g24: rule_code | rule_bash | rule_media
rule__dictate: WORD+
%import common.WORD
%ignore " "
start: (rule_code | rule_media | rule_bash | rule_terminal | rule_optional_number | rule_cmdprompt | rule_windows)~1..99

Trying to parse this times out, while switching the start "~1..99" to "+" allows it to parse almost instantly. However, it would be nice to be able to match larger repetitions starting at numbers other than 0 or 1 (like {m,} in regex), as well as fixed higher repetitions (~10 also timed out). Is there any way some of these cases could be optimized?

Thanks for the amazing library!

Edit: Just to clarify, this is referring to initializing an instance of the Lark class.
